sections.heading: Usage
sections.body:
To retrieve paginated results, perform the following steps:

- [create a MongoDB Search index](ref-create-index) on the fields you want to query.

- Run a [$search](pipe.$search) query that returns a point of reference. To
  learn more, see [Retrieve Point of Reference](paginate-results-search-sequence-token).

- Use the point of reference in your subsequent [$search](pipe.$search)
  query to retrieve the next or previous set of documents in the
  results.

  - To learn more about retrieving results to build a "Next
    Page" function, see [retrieve search results after a reference point](paginate-results-search-after).

  - To learn more about retrieving results to build a "Previous Page"
    function, see [Search Before a Specific Point of Reference](paginate-results-search-before).

  - To jump to a page in your results, combine [$skip](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/skip) and
    [$limit](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/limit) with [$search](pipe.$search) `searchAfter` or
    `searchBefore` options. For example, to jump to results from Page
    3 to Page 5, with 10 results per page, do the following:

    - Retrieve results using `searchAfter` with the point of
      reference for the last result on Page 3 (Result 30).

    - Use [$skip](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/skip) to skip the 10 results on Page 4 (Results
      31-40) and [$limit](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/limit) to limit the results to 10
      documents.

    - Return results for Page 5 (Results 41-50).

    Here, using [$skip](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/skip) with the `searchAfter` option optimizes
    the query to skip only 1 page of results (10 documents). By comparison,
    if you use `$skip` without the [$search](pipe.$search) `searchAfter`
    option, the query skips 4 pages of results (40 documents). To learn
    more, see [Jump from Page 2 to Page 5 Using searchAfter and $skip](sequential-pagination-tutorial-skip-query).

sections.heading: Considerations
sections.body:
- A tie occurs when you sort on a field for which multiple documents
  have identical values. MongoDB doesn't guarantee ordering of tied query
  results, which can lead to duplication and inconsistency when you use
  `searchAfter` and `searchBefore`. Apply the following principles to
  reduce relevance score ties:

  - Sort your query by a unique field to prevent relevance score ties. For
    an example, see [Sort by Score And a Unique Field](fts-sort-score-unique-field).

  - If you want to primarily sort by a non-unique field, add a secondary
    sort clause on a unique field to serve as a tiebreaker.

- Updating or deleting documents between queries might cause
  inconsistencies in result order. Apply the following principles
  to support deterministic search behavior:

  - Sort your query results by an immutable field, such as `_id`. MongoDB Search
    reflects the updates you make to your collection between initial and
    subsequent queries. If you sort by a mutable field such as
    `updated_time` and you update your collection between your first and
    second queries, MongoDB Search might order the same documents differently.

  - If you deployed [dedicated MongoDB Search nodes](node-architecture) and
    you are sorting results by `searchScore`, consider the following:

    - By default, MongoDB Search scores documents using the `bm25` similarity
      algorithm, which calculates term frequency in relation to the entire
      corpus of documents on the MongoDB Search node. Because each MongoDB Search node
      replicates from the changestream independently, this corpus might vary
      across MongoDB Search nodes. As a result, the same query can return different
      `bm25` scores when routed to different MongoDB Search nodes. Subsequent
      queries are more likely to be routed to different MongoDB Search nodes if your
      deployment uses [dedicated MongoDB Search nodes](node-architecture), or
      your [read preference](manual/reference/read-preference) is
      set to `secondary` or `nearest`.

    - To ensure consistent scores across subsequent queries, set the
      `similarity.type` property to `stableTfl` or `boolean` when you
      index a field as the MongoDB Search [string](bson-data-types-string) or
      [tokenization](bson-data-types-autocomplete) type. This forces
      the [text](text-ref), [Phrase](phrase-ref),
      [queryString](querystring-ref), and [autocomplete](autocomplete-ref) operators to use the `stableTfl` or `boolean`
      similarity algorithm to calculate relevance scores for queries on the
      indexed field. These algorithms calculate scores consistently across
      all MongoDB Search nodes. To learn more, see [Score Details](fts-similarity-algorithms).

- To count search results during pagination, use the [count](count-ref) option in your initial [$search](pipe.$search) query or in a
  separate [$searchMeta](pipe.$searchMeta) query. This method is more efficient
  than using the [$count](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/count) stage after your [$search](pipe.$search)
  stage.

  The [count](count-ref) option returns the total number of
  documents that match your search criteria, so you don't need to use
  the `count` option in later queries if you are generating paginated
  results. To keep counting separate from your main `$search` query,
  use a [$searchMeta](pipe.$searchMeta) stage with the `count` option.

  Counting search results can affect performance. Use the [count](count-ref) option only when needed, for example, when retrieving
  search results for the first page.

sections.heading: Retrieve Point of Reference
sections.body:
To retrieve query results at a certain point, you must provide the point
of reference in your [$search](pipe.$search) query. You can retrieve the
reference point by using the [$meta](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/meta) keyword
`searchSequenceToken` in the [$project](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/project) stage after your
[$search](pipe.$search) stage.

sections.heading: Output
sections.body:
The `searchSequenceToken` generates a Base64-encoded token for each
document in the results. The length of the token increases with the number
of fields specified in the [sorting](sort-ref) option of your query.
The token isn't tied to a snapshot of the database.

The documents in the results are sorted in the default order, unless you
specify the `sort` option in your query. To learn about sorting your
results, see [sorting](sort-ref).

sections.heading: Search After a Specific Point of Reference
sections.body:
To search after a reference point, you must specify the reference point
in your [$search](pipe.$search) query by using the `searchAfter` option with
the token generated by `searchSequenceToken`. You can use the token
generated by `searchSequenceToken` only when you rerun the
[$search](pipe.$search) query for which `searchSequenceToken` generated
the token. The semantics (search fields and values) of the subsequent
[$search](pipe.$search) query in which you use the token must be identical
to the query for which `searchSequenceToken` generated the token.

You can use the `searchAfter` option to build a "Next Page" function
in your application. For a demonstration of this, see the [examples](search-before-egs) on this page.

sections.heading: Output
sections.body:
MongoDB Search returns the documents in the results after the specified token.
MongoDB Search returns the generated tokens for the documents in the results
because you specified the `searchSequenceToken` in the
[$project](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/project) stage after the [$search](pipe.$search) stage (as
shown in line 11). These tokens can be used as a reference point for
another query with the same semantics.

The documents in the results are sorted in the default order, unless you
specify the `sort` option in your query. To learn about sorting your
results, see [sorting](sort-ref).

sections.heading: Search Before a Specific Point of Reference
sections.body:
To search before a reference point, you must specify the reference point
in your [$search](pipe.$search) query by using the `searchBefore` option
with the token generated by `searchSequenceToken`. You can use the token
generated by `searchSequenceToken` only when you rerun the
[$search](pipe.$search) query for which `searchSequenceToken` generated
the token. The semantics (search fields and values) of the subsequent
[$search](pipe.$search) query in which you use the token must be identical
to the query for which `searchSequenceToken` generated the token.

You can use the `searchBefore` option to build a "Previous Page"
function in your application. To do this, combine the following:

- [$search](pipe.$search) stage with `searchBefore` option

- [$limit](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/limit) stage

- [toArray()](manual/reference/method/cursor.toArray) and
  `reverse()` methods.

For a demonstration of this, see the `searchBefore` query [examples](search-before-egs) on this page.

sections.heading: searchBefore Output
sections.body:
MongoDB Search returns documents in the results preceding the specified token in
reverse order. MongoDB Search also returns the generated tokens for the documents
in the results because you specified the `searchSequenceToken` in the
[$project](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/project) stage after the [$search](pipe.$search) stage (as
shown in line 11). These tokens can be used as a reference point for
another query with the same semantics.
